candidate_problems.text: How can 'combinatorial expressiveness of the genotype-to-phenotype mapping' be defined and measured in a way that is non-circular, comparable across taxa, and empirically separable from observed organismal complexity itself?

What are the actual bounds linking regulatory/developmental architecture to realized organismal complexity once environmental input, stochasticity, and historical constraints are included—i.e., is there any robust invariant or upper bound at all, or does context-dependence dissolve the conjecture into a family of local models?

Which aspects of regulatory topology are genuinely causal leverage points for complexity growth versus merely correlated byproducts of evolution, and how can interventions or comparative models distinguish architecture-driven generative power from selection history and phylogenetic contingency?
